front.title: Portable Revocation Statements for Digest-Addressed Authorization Artifacts

abstract.t: Signed authorization artifacts for agent actions (receipts, commits, delegations) are being defined faster than the means to retract them. In deployed systems revocation is server state: a record is flipped in a live datastore, and a relying party must call back to the issuer to learn of it. There is no portable artifact a relying party can be handed to prove, offline, that a previously valid authorization has been revoked. This document defines the revocation statement: a signed, offline-verifiable claim that a named target, addressed by identifier and content digest, is revoked. Verification is fail-closed and evaluates a fixed set of checks: version, exact target binding (both the identifier and the digest), a revoker key pinned by the verifier (a self-asserted key confers nothing), the presence of a revocation instant, an independently recomputed signature, and an optional bounded-staleness window. The statement proves that a named, pinned revoker revoked this specific target. It does not prove that every relying party saw it, and offline verification cannot prove the absence of a later revocation; bounded staleness is the stated mitigation, not a substitute for a revocation feed.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: The evidence stack for high-risk agent actions produces durable signed artifacts: authorization receipts , policy commits, delegation records. Each is designed to be carried, presented, and verified offline, without a call back to its issuer. Their retraction is not. Revocation, where it exists, is a server-side status flag: the issuer's datastore records that an authorization is revoked, and only parties who query that datastore learn of it. A relying party holding the artifact but not a connection to the issuer has no way to be shown that the artifact was revoked, and a revoker has no portable proof that it revoked.

Recent gap analyses in the agent-authorization space name artifact-level revocation as an unmet need: the field defines new signed objects readily but leaves their lifecycle end unspecified. This document fills that specific slot. It defines a revocation statement, a small signed object that binds one exact target, carries who revoked it, when, and why, and verifies offline under a key the verifier has pinned in advance. The statement is deliberately the same shape as the artifacts it retracts: portable, digest-bound, and verifiable with no network.

The scope discipline of this document is strict. A revocation statement is the analogue of a CRL entry or an OCSP response , not of the CRL or OCSP service: it proves that a specific revocation is real and binds a specific target. It does not, and cannot, tell a relying party about revocations it was never handed. states plainly what verification proves and what it does not.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

Target: the artifact being revoked, addressed by a type, an identifier, and the content digest of the action the artifact committed to. Revoker: the party asserting the revocation. Relying party: the party deciding whether to honor the artifact the statement concerns. Pinned key: a public key the relying party associated with a revoker identifier out of band, before verification.

section.name: The Revocation Statement (EP-REVOCATION-v1)
section.t: A revocation statement is a JSON object:
section.sourcecode: { "@version": "EP-REVOCATION-v1", "target_type": "receipt", "target_id": "rcpt_01J...", "action_hash": "sha256:<hex>", "revoker_id": "ep:key:revoker#1", "revoked_at": "2026-06-14T20:41:00Z", "reason": "key compromise suspected", "proof": { "algorithm": "Ed25519", "revoker_key_id": "ep:key:revoker#1", "signed_payload_b64u": "<base64url canonical payload>", "signature_b64u": "<base64url Ed25519 signature>", "public_key": "<base64url SPKI DER>" } }

section.name: Target
section.t: target_type names the kind of artifact being revoked. This version recognizes exactly three values: "receipt", "commit", and "delegation". target_id is the identifier of the artifact. action_hash is the content digest the artifact committed to: a sha256 digest, carried either as bare lowercase hex or with a "sha256:" prefix.

Both target_id AND action_hash are part of the binding. A statement revokes the (target_type, target_id, action_hash) triple, never just an identifier. Binding only an identifier would let a revocation minted for one action be replayed against a reissued artifact carrying the same identifier but a different action; the digest closes that.

section.name: Revoker, Instant, and Reason
section.t: revoker_id names the party asserting the revocation. The verifier resolves this identifier against its own pinned key registry (); the identifier alone confers nothing. revoked_at is the instant the revocation took effect, as an timestamp. It is REQUIRED: it is the anchor for the staleness bound, and a statement without it is refused. reason is a human-readable cause; it MAY be null. Because reason and revoked_at are inside the signed payload, neither can be edited after signing.

section.name: Proof
section.t: proof carries the revoker's detached signature. algorithm is "Ed25519" . revoker_key_id names the signing key. public_key is the revoker's public key as base64url of the SPKI DER encoding. signature_b64u is the base64url of a detached Ed25519 signature over the signed payload defined in . signed_payload_b64u is the base64url of the bytes the producer signed; it is carried for diagnostics only, and a verifier MUST NOT use it: the verifier recomputes the signed payload from the presented fields ().

A statement without a proof is a bare claim. It MUST be refused by verifiers: no signature verifies, so the signature checks fail closed. A producer MUST NOT emit a statement that lacks any of target_type, target_id, action_hash, revoker_id, revoked_at, or a signature; the reference implementation's builder refuses to mint one.

A statement MAY additionally carry informational members outside the signed field set (the reference implementation appends a scope_note restating the boundary in ). Such members carry no verification semantics.

section.name: Signed Payload and Canonicalization
section.t: The revoker's signature is bound to the canonical bytes of a fixed field set, SIGNED_FIELDS: the object containing exactly "@version", "target_type", "target_id", "action_hash", "revoker_id", "revoked_at", and "reason", with any absent member replaced by null. The canonical serialization is deterministic JSON: object members sorted lexicographically by key at every nesting level, no insignificant whitespace, standard JSON string escaping. For the value domain a revocation statement uses (strings and null), this serialization agrees with the JSON Canonicalization Scheme . The signed bytes are the UTF-8 encoding of that serialization.

section.name: Verification (Fail-Closed)
section.t: Verification takes three inputs: the target the relying party holds, the candidate statement, and the relying party's options (its pinned revoker key registry and an optional staleness bound). The target MUST be derived from the artifact the relying party holds, never taken from the statement: the statement is the claim under test, not the source of what it is tested against.

The verifier evaluates seven named checks. The verdict is valid if and only if every check passes; any single failure yields an invalid verdict. Failure is a structured refusal carrying the failed checks and reasons, never an exception: malformed, missing, or non-canonicalizable input produces a refusal, not a crash.

dl.dd: The statement's "@version" MUST equal "EP-REVOCATION-v1". A statement under any other tag may carry different semantics and is refused.

The statement MUST bind the exact target the verifier holds. If no target is supplied, or the held target's target_type is not one of the three recognized values, the check fails. Otherwise the statement's target_type and target_id MUST equal the held target's, and the statement's action_hash MUST equal the held target's action_hash after normalization (an optional "sha256:" prefix is stripped and hex is compared case-insensitively). A statement whose target_id matches but whose action_hash differs is the revoke-A-presented-for-B case and MUST be refused: revoking A never revokes B.

The statement's revoker_id MUST resolve to a public key the verifier pinned in advance. No pinned entry (or no registry at all) means refusal: a self-asserted key confers nothing, since anyone can mint a keypair and sign "X is revoked". If the proof carries a public_key, it MUST equal the pinned key; a differing key is a key-substitution attempt and is refused. The verifier MUST NOT fall back to the proof's own public_key.
revoked_at MUST be present and parse as a well-formed timestamp. A statement without a revocation instant has no staleness anchor and no record of when the revocation took effect, and is refused.
The proof's signature MUST verify, under the PINNED key and no other, over the payload bytes the verifier itself recomputes per . A missing proof, missing signature, or forged signature is refused.

The bytes the signature is checked against are recomputed from the presented statement fields; a producer-supplied payload blob is never used. If any signed field (the target, revoker_id, revoked_at, or reason) was edited after signing, the recomputed bytes differ from the signed bytes and the statement is refused. If the presented fields cannot be canonicalized, no payload bytes exist and no signature can verify against them: refusal, fail-closed. If no statement is presented at all, this check and the previous one both fail: an absent statement confers nothing.

Evaluated only when the relying party supplies a maximum age. The verifier computes the age of revoked_at relative to a caller-supplied reference time (defaulting to the current time) and refuses a statement older than the maximum. When no maximum age is supplied, this check passes vacuously. This bound applies to a PRESENTED statement only; see for what it does not do.

section.name: Aggregation over Multiple Statements
section.t: A relying party may have collected several statements from several sources. The aggregate rule is: the target is revoked, as far as this collection shows, if and only if at least one statement in the collection verifies against the target under . The matching statement need not be first or alone; statements that are validly signed but bind other targets are ignored, not errors. Anything that is not a collection yields false.
The aggregate rule is not a completeness oracle. A false result means "no valid binding statement is present in this collection", never "this target was never revoked anywhere".

section.name: Generalization: Any Digest-Addressed Artifact
section.t: Nothing in the statement or its verification is specific to one artifact kind. The binding is the (target_type, target_id, action_hash) triple, so the mechanism applies to any artifact addressed by an identifier plus a content digest: this version enumerates authorization receipts, commits, and delegation records as the recognized target types. A verifier conforming to this version refuses a held target of any other type; extending the recognized set is a matter for a future version, not for unilateral verifier behavior.

section.name: What a Revocation Statement Proves and Does Not Prove
section.t: Offline verification of a revocation statement proves, with no network and no trust in the bearer:

ul.li: a named revoker, whose key the relying party pinned, signed the revocation (attribution, not authority);
the statement binds the exact (target_type, target_id, action_hash) the relying party is reasoning about;
the signed fields, including revoked_at and reason, were not edited after signing; and
when the relying party requires it, the statement is no older than its staleness bound.

section.t: It does NOT prove:

ul.li: that every relying party saw the revocation. A statement proves the revoker revoked; distribution is a separate problem, and a revoker holding a valid statement has proof it acted, not proof anyone received it.

the absence of a later revocation. Offline verification cannot answer "has this authorization been revoked by a statement I do not hold?"; that is the liveness and transparency problem that CRLs and OCSP exist to address, and it is out of scope here. Treating absence-of-statement as proof-of-not-revoked is a relying-party error this mechanism cannot prevent. Bounded staleness is the mitigation this document offers, and it is a bound on presented statements only: a relying party that needs liveness MUST consult a revocation feed or transparency log, or rely on a short validity lifetime on the underlying artifact.

that the revoker was entitled to revoke this target. The signature proves who signed and what it binds; whether that party had the authority to revoke is the relying party's pinning policy (whom it pins as a revoker for a given target), not a property of the statement.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: Pinning is the trust boundary. The only trust input this mechanism adds is the revoker's signature under a key the verifier pinned out of band. Every attack in the conformance catalogue that involves keys reduces to one rule: the verdict honors the pinned key and nothing else. An unpinned revoker is refused even when its embedded key verifies its own signature; a pinned revoker presenting a different key is refused even when the signature math passes under the presented key.

Recompute, never trust. The verifier derives the signed bytes from the presented fields and ignores the producer-supplied payload blob. A verifier that checked the signature against the carried blob would accept a statement whose visible fields were edited after signing.

Bind the digest, not just the identifier. The revoke-A-presented-for-B replay, where a genuine revocation for one action is stapled to another artifact, is refused because action_hash is part of the binding and of the signed bytes.

Fail closed means refuse with a reason. Malformed statements, absent statements, unparseable timestamps, and non-canonicalizable fields all produce a structured refusal naming the failed check; verification never throws and never degrades to acceptance.

A revocation statement grants nothing. A valid statement only retracts; it cannot be replayed to authorize, and a forged one fails signature verification. The residual harm of a forged statement that somehow verified would be denial of service (treating a live authorization as revoked), which is why the pinning rule and the exact-binding rule both gate acceptance.

Freshness is not completeness. The staleness bound rejects old presented statements; it does nothing about statements never presented. Deployments MUST NOT advertise the bound as a revocation-checking guarantee; is normative reading for anyone relying on this mechanism.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions. A future revision may register a media type for the statement.

front.title: Authorization Receipts for High-Risk Agent Actions

section.name: Implementation Status
section.t: A reference implementation is published Apache-2.0 in the EMILIA Protocol repository (lib/revocation/revocation.js: building, single-statement verification with the seven named checks in the order specified here, and the aggregate rule over a collection). An attack-catalogue-first conformance vector file (conformance/vectors/revocation.v1.json) enumerates nine rejection cases every conformant verifier is required to refuse (forged signature; unpinned revoker key; substituted pinned key; different target_id; different action_hash; wrong version; fields tampered after signing; missing revoked_at; stale beyond the staleness bound) and two acceptance cases (a well-formed binding statement, and the aggregate rule finding one valid binding statement among unrelated ones). The negative vectors are minted with real keys and real detached signatures over canonical bytes, so each is a genuine forgery attempt rather than hand-edited JSON that fails for an unrelated reason.
